    Home › Aircraft › Airbus A300

    Airbus A300

    Airbus · Widebody · Out of production (2007) — in service

    The first Airbus.

    Length54.08 m
    Wingspan44.84 m
    Range7,500 km
    MTOW172 t
    Typical seats266
    Built561
    Our numbers

    Derived metrics

    Computed by aircraft.fyi from the core specification — see how we calculate these.

    Seats per metre#9 of 30
    4.92 seats/m typical seats ÷ fuselage length
    Range per tonne#20 of 40
    43.7 km/t maximum range ÷ MTOW in tonnes
    Span-to-length ratio#34 of 43
    0.83 wingspan ÷ fuselage length
    Weight per metre#27 of 43
    3.17 t/m MTOW ÷ fuselage length
    Range in hours#24 of 40
    8.4 h maximum range ÷ cruise speed
    Weight per seat#19 of 30
    645 kg MTOW ÷ typical seats
    Sense of scale

    How big is it, really?

    The Airbus A300's wingspan, against things you already know the size of.

    Airbus A30044.84 m
    Football pitch (width)68 m
    Blue whale25 m
    New Routemaster bus11.2 m
    You (probably)1.75 m
    #29 of 43 by length → #30 by weight → #26 by speed →
    Specification

    The numbers

    Length (A300-600)54.08 m
    Wingspan44.84 m
    Height16.62 m
    MTOW (-600R)171,700 kg
    Range (-600R)7,500 km
    Typical seats266 (2-class)
    Engines2 × GE CF6-80C2 or PW4158
    First flight28 October 1972
    Built561

    Compare it

    Put the Airbus A300 next to something

    vs Ilyushin Il-86 →vs Boeing 767 →
    vs Airbus A330 vs Boeing 767 Compare with anything →
    ← PreviousAirbus A220 Next →Airbus A320 family

    Last verified: 2026-07-14 · Spot an error? business@luck.fyi

    The story

    What makes it different

    Every Airbus traces back to this one. The A300 was the bet a new European consortium made in 1972: the world's first twin-engine widebody, at a time when Boeing said widebodies needed three or four engines. Early sales were so bad Airbus lent aircraft to Eastern Air Lines for free — then the fuel-efficient twin logic became the entire future of the industry.

    The A300-600R matured it, and its cross-section proved perfect for containers: FedEx and UPS built enormous fleets, and the A300-600ST Beluga carried Airbus's own parts for 25 years.

    Passenger A300s are nearly gone, but the freighters thunder on every night — over a hundred still working the parcel networks, half a century after the aircraft that started everything first flew.

    Variants

    The Airbus A300 line

    A300B2 / B4
    The originals — the world's first widebody twin.
    A300-600 / -600R
    The definitive version. Spec shown above.
    A300-600ST Beluga
    The original outsize transporter.
    A300F / P2F
    The freighters that keep the type alive.
    Operators

    Who flies the Airbus A300

    The top 4 operators by fleet size, of ≈15 worldwide — the rest are aggregated below. Counts marked ≈ are approximate, verified 2026-07-14.

    FXFedEx ExpressThe largest A300 fleet in the world — all -600 freighters.≈65→ 5XUPS AirlinesA300-600Fs bought new — the last A300s ever built.52→
    DEEuropean Air Transport (DHL)≈20
    IRIranian operatorsA handful of passenger A300s remain in service.≈15
    Other operatorsA tail of freight, charter and government operators worldwide.≈30

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
